questions.question: How many syndrome defects does a single X error create?
questions.explanation: A single X error on an edge creates exactly two Z-syndrome defects, one at each of the two Z-plaquettes that share that edge. This is because the error anticommutes with exactly two Z-stabilizers.

questions.question: What happens when two adjacent X errors occur?
questions.explanation: Two adjacent X errors share a Z-stabilizer in the middle. The syndrome contributions cancel there (XOR logic), leaving only two defects at the ends of the error chain. This is why chains can be inferred from their endpoints.

questions.question: A Y error on a single qubit creates how many syndrome defects total?
questions.explanation: Y = iXZ, so a Y error combines an X error and a Z error. The X component creates 2 Z-syndrome defects, and the Z component creates 2 X-syndrome defects, for a total of 4 defects.

questions.question: What determines whether an error chain causes a logical error?

options.text: Whether the chain spans the code (connects opposite boundaries)
The number of defects it creates
The time at which it occurred

questions.explanation: An error chain causes a logical error if it spans the code from one boundary type to its opposite (e.g., rough to rough for Z errors). This creates a non-trivial cycle homologous to the logical operator, flipping the logical state.

questions.question: Why do syndrome defects always come in even numbers (in the bulk)?

options.text: Because syndromes are the boundary of error chains, and boundaries are closed
Because X and Z errors are equally likely

questions.explanation: Syndrome defects mark the endpoints (boundary) of error chains. Since every edge in a chain contributes to exactly two vertices, the total parity of syndrome values is even. This is a topological property.

questions.question: In the 3D syndrome structure (space × time), what does a temporal defect pair indicate?
questions.explanation: A defect that appears and disappears at the same spatial location (paired in time) indicates a measurement error. The defect is transient and doesn't correspond to a persistent data error, so no data correction is needed.

questions.question: A single syndrome defect (odd number) can appear when an error chain terminates at a code boundary.
questions.explanation: True. At boundaries, one endpoint of an error chain can be 'absorbed' by the boundary (which acts as a virtual defect), leaving an odd number of visible defects in the bulk. The decoder treats boundaries as valid matching endpoints.
